title: Chemical Bonding & Molecular Structure Test - 4 (Topic Test)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: SONAR is a device that is used to measure the distance of underwater objects by a ship. Which of the following types of waves does it use for this purpose?
options: Sound waves in audible range for human beings
questions.solution:
SONAR, which stands for Sound Navigation and Ranging, is a device used to measure the distance of underwater objects. The correct type of waves that SONAR uses for this purpose is:

Option C: Ultrasonic waves

Ultrasonic waves are sound waves with frequencies higher than the audible range for human beings (greater than 20 kHz). These waves are particularly effective for underwater detection because they can travel long distances with high precision and can easily reflect off objects, providing accurate distance measurements.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following wavelength radiations may correspond to an X-ray ?
questions.solution:
X-rays have very short wavelengths, usually in the range of about

$$ 0.01 \text{ nm to } 10 \text{ nm} $$

Now check the options:

  • $1 \text{ nm}$: lies in the X-ray range

  • $10 \text{ nm}$: at the extreme end, but usually not taken as the standard answer here

  • $100 \text{ nm}$: ultraviolet region

  • $300 \text{ nm}$: ultraviolet region

So, the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{1 \text{ nm}} $$

Option 1.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: A 100 g sphere is moving at a speed of 20 m/s and collides with another. sphere of mass 50 g. If the second sphere was at rest prior to the collision and the first sphere comes at rest immediately after the collision, considering the collision to be elastic, the speed of the second sphere would be
questions.solution:
To determine the speed of the second sphere after an elastic collision, we can use the principle of conservation of momentum and the fact that kinetic energy is conserved in an elastic collision. Let's denote the masses and velocities of the spheres as follows:

Mass of the first sphere, $$m_1 = 100 \, \text{g} = 0.1 \, \text{kg}$$

Initial velocity of the first sphere, $$u_1 = 20 \, \text{m/s}$$

Mass of the second sphere, $$m_2 = 50 \, \text{g} = 0.05 \, \text{kg}$$

Initial velocity of the second sphere, $$u_2 = 0 \, \text{m/s}$$

Final velocity of the first sphere, $$v_1 = 0 \, \text{m/s}$$

We need to find the final velocity of the second sphere, $$v_2$$.

Using the conservation of momentum:

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$$

Substituting the given values:

$$0.1 \times 20 + 0.05 \times 0 = 0.1 \times 0 + 0.05 \times v_2$$

$$2 = 0.05 \times v_2$$

Solving for $$v_2$$:

$$v_2 = \frac{2}{0.05} = 40 \, \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the speed of the second sphere after the collision is 40 m/s. The correct answer is:

Option D

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: If three resistors of 1 Ohm each connect in parallel to each other the resultant resistance is
questions.solution:
For resistors in parallel, the reciprocal of the total resistance is the sum of reciprocals:

$$ \frac1{R_{\rm eq}} = \frac1{R_1} + \frac1{R_2} + \frac1{R_3} = \frac1{1} + \frac1{1} + \frac1{1} = 3 $$

So

$$ R_{\rm eq} = \frac1{3}\,\Omega. $$

That corresponds to Option 2: 1/3 Ohm.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Twinkling of stars is due to
options: reflection of starlight from the oceanic surface
questions.solution:
The phenomenon of twinkling of stars is due to the atmospheric refraction of starlight.

When starlight enters the Earth's atmosphere, it passes through layers of air at different temperatures and densities. As light travels through these varying layers, it is continuously refracted, or bent, in different directions. This refraction causes the light of the star to fluctuate in intensity and position as observed from Earth, leading to the perception of the star "twinkling." This effect is more pronounced for stars near the horizon compared to those directly overhead because the light path through the atmosphere is longer and passes through more layers of varying density.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The composition of gases in exosphere is
questions.solution:
The exosphere is primarily composed of light gases. Helium and hydrogen are the two main constituents found in this outermost layer of Earth's atmosphere.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: Helium and Hydrogen.

questions.questionText: A ball is thrown vertically upward with a speed of 40 m/s. The time taken by the ball to reach the maximum height would be approximately

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Sita, 1.5 m high, stands before a plane mirror fixed on a wall to view her full image. What should be the minimum height of the plane mirror so that Sita can view her image fully?
questions.solution:
To determine the minimum height of the plane mirror required for Sita to view her full image, we need to understand the properties of a plane mirror. A plane mirror reflects light in such a way that the image formed is virtual, upright, and of the same size as the object. In order for her to see her complete image, the length of the mirror must be half the height of Sita.

Here's why: When Sita looks at her reflection in the mirror, the topmost and bottommost rays from her body will reflect off the mirror at equal angles. To capture both her head and feet in the reflection, the mirror needs to be at least half her height. This is because each point on Sita's body is reflected in the corresponding point on the mirror at half the height, allowing the complete image to fit.

So, if Sita's height is 1.5 m, the minimum height of the mirror should be:

$$\frac{1.5 \, \text{m}}{2} = 0.75 \, \text{m}$$

Thus, the correct option is:

Option C: 0.75 m

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Shown in the figure are two hollow cubes C1 and C2 of negligible mass partially filled (depicted by darkened area) with liquids of densities ρ1 and ρ2, respectively, floating in water (density ρw). The relationship between ρ1, ρ2 and ρw is
options: ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw
ρ1 < ρw < ρ2
questions.solution:
Key Idea:

When something floats, it means it is lighter (has a lower average density) than the liquid it is in.

For floating, the weight of water pushed aside by the object is equal to the weight of the object.

If an object sinks deeper (is more submerged), it means its average density is higher.

For hollow cubes with very small weight, the heaviness (density) of the cube comes only from the liquid that is inside them.

What the Figure Shows:

The figure has two cubes, C1 and C2, each filled with a different liquid. The densities of these liquids are ρ1 and ρ2.

C2 is deeper in the water than C1. This means C2 is heavier (has a greater density) than C1.

Because the cubes themselves do not weigh much, the only thing that affects how deep they float is the density of the liquid inside.

So, the density ρ1 (inside C1) is greater than ρ2 (inside C2).

Both cubes float. This tells us that the density of the liquids inside must be less than the density of water (ρw), or otherwise they would sink.

Putting all this together:

ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw

Answer: ρ2 < ρ1 < ρw

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText:
A metallic bob X of mass m is released from position A. It collides elastically with another identical bob Y placed at rest at position B on a horizontal frictionless table. The angle AOB is 30°.

How high does the bob X rise immediately after the collision?
options: To the same height as that of position A on the other side in the same trajectory
To half the height as that of position A on the other side along the same trajectory
The same height at position A
questions.solution:
To solve this problem, we need to consider the principles of conservation of momentum and conservation of energy. The collision between the two bobs is elastic, meaning both kinetic energy and momentum are conserved.

First, let's denote the initial height from which the bob X is released as $$h$$. When bob X is released from position A, it converts its potential energy into kinetic energy just before the collision :

$$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Solving for the velocity $$v$$ just before collision :

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

Since the bobs are identical (same mass $$m$$) and the collision is elastic, the velocities of the bobs after the collision can be derived using the following properties of an elastic collision: the moving object will come to rest, and the object at rest will move with the velocity of the moving object. Therefore, after the collision, the bob Y will move with the initial velocity of bob X, and bob X will come to a stop.

Because bob X comes to a stop immediately after the collision, it does not rise to any height after the collision. Hence, the correct answer is :

Option D : It stops at position B

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: <!-- x-tinymce/html -->A car weighs 1000 kg. It is moving with a uniform velocity of 72 km/h towards a straight road. The driver suddenly presses the brakes. The car stops in 0.2 s. The retarding force applied on the car to stop it is
questions.solution:
Convert the speed to m/s:

$$v = 72\ \mathrm{km/h} = 72\cdot\frac{1000}{3600}\ \mathrm{m/s} = 20\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

Compute the deceleration (since it goes from 20 m/s to 0 in 0.2 s):

$$a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{0 - 20}{0.2} = -100\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

Find the retarding force using Newton’s second law:

$$F = m\,a = 1000\ \mathrm{kg}\times(-100\ \mathrm{m/s^2}) = -100\,000\ \mathrm N$$

The magnitude is $100{,}000\ \mathrm N$, i.e. 100 kN.

Answer: Option 4, 100 kN.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The focal length of a concave lens is 0.5 m. The power of the lens is
questions.solution:
For a lens, power is given by

$$ P = \frac{1}{f} $$

where $f$ is in metre.

Since it is a concave lens, its focal length is negative:

$$ f = -0.5 \, \text{m} $$

Now,

$$ P = \frac{1}{-0.5} = -2 \, \text{D} $$

So, the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{-2.0 \, \text{D}} $$

Option 4

questions.subject: Chemistry
options: only total internal reflection of light.
both refraction and total internal reflection of light.
questions.solution:
A mirage is an illustration of both refraction and total internal reflection of light.

Explanation:

Mirages occur when layers of air at different temperatures cause light to bend. This is primarily due to refraction, which is the bending of light as it passes from one medium to another with a different density.

In the case of a hot road, the air near the surface is significantly warmer than the air above it. Light from the sky bends as it moves through these layers of varying density, and in some cases, this bending can continue until the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle for total internal reflection.

Key Concepts:

Refraction: Occurs when light waves pass from one medium to another and change speed, causing the light to bend. This bending creates the initial path alteration of light.

Total Internal Reflection: After sufficient bending, if light strikes the boundary at a specific angle (greater than the critical angle), it reflects entirely back into the medium instead of passing through.

Together, refraction and total internal reflection help explain why a mirage makes a distant object, like the sky, appear as though it is reflecting off the ground, creating the illusion of water. Hence, Option D - both refraction and total internal reflection of light - is correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: A microscope may be a combination of :
options: a convex lens and a convex mirror.
questions.solution:
A microscope is typically a combination of two convex lenses. The basic design of a simple microscope, also known as a magnifying glass, uses a single convex lens. However, compound microscopes, which are more common in scientific applications, use two convex lenses arranged in series to achieve higher magnifying power.

Each lens in a compound microscope serves a specific purpose:

Objective Lens: This is the lens closest to the object being viewed. It gathers light from the specimen and creates a enlarged real image.

Eyepiece Lens (Ocular Lens): This lens is closest to the observer's eye. It magnifies the real image created by the objective lens to create a larger virtual image that the observer can see.

The arrangement of these two lenses allows for greater magnification and resolution compared to a single lens. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: two convex lenses.
